title: The Path to Sovereign Data: Challenges and Priorities in Local-First Computing
gist: At a Local First Conf 2026 Berlin panel, speakers from Scuttlebutt, Bluesky, Ink & Switch, and Supramundane argued data ownership requires usable mechanisms beyond formal legal rights, with Bluesky's Paul Frazee acknowledging AT Protocol shifted from pure peer-to-peer toward server-based infrastructure specifically to support an Internet-scale (240M DAU) social network -- an explicit admission that pure decentralization doesn't scale to mainstream platforms.

themes.theme: Data ownership requires usable mechanisms, not just formal rights

insights.claim: Bluesky's Paul Frazee argued that formal data-ownership rights without usable mechanisms are insufficient, citing GDPR-style data access requests as an example where users can legally obtain their data but often only in forms that are technically inaccessible to ordinary people.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: A direct critique that regulatory data-portability frameworks like GDPR often fail in practice due to unusable data export formats, relevant to evaluating the real-world effectiveness of data-rights regulation versus product-level solutions.
insights.locator: Frazee argued that formal rights without usable mechanisms were insufficient

insights.claim: Panelists Zenna Fiscella of Scuttlebutt and Boris Mann of Ink & Switch framed data ownership as requiring structural independence from vertically integrated platforms, with Berjon arguing unbundling platforms is both a technical and political objective and Mann framing the long-term goal as making systems fit into existing social and local contexts rather than forcing users into centralized infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Positions data ownership as a systems/governance question rather than a single product feature, relevant to evaluating the strategic direction of the broader local-first software movement.
insights.locator: A recurring theme was that local-first systems would need to move beyond vertically integrated platforms

insights.claim: Bluesky's Paul Frazee described AT Protocol's architectural shift from a peer-to-peer design toward server-based infrastructure as a deliberate move to support large-scale social networking demands, citing a 240 million daily active user target associated with Twitter-scale operation as the reason pure decentralization was set aside.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: An explicit, named admission from a Bluesky co-founder that pure peer-to-peer decentralization does not scale to mainstream social-network usage levels -- important nuance for evaluating decentralization-focused pitches and the practical ceiling on 'fully decentralized' social infrastructure claims.
insights.locator: Frazee described AT Protocol's shift from peer-to-peer architecture

insights.claim: Bluesky's stated goal is to bootstrap an ecosystem around AT Protocol that could eventually outlive the company itself, an explicit design intent for protocol durability independent of the corporate entity.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Relevant to long-term platform-risk assessment of Bluesky/AT Protocol as infrastructure: the company is explicitly designing for its own eventual obsolescence relative to the protocol, which changes how dependent third-party developers should weigh company-specific risk.
insights.locator: He said Bluesky's stated goal was to bootstrap an ecosystem

insights.claim: Panelists identified interoperability bridges between protocols and identity systems as a major missing infrastructure layer, arguing different communities will continue to need different identity/governance models and that reusable bridges -- rather than one universal identity or trust model -- are needed to enable cross-ecosystem application reuse.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Identifies a concrete infrastructure gap (protocol/identity bridges) in the local-first ecosystem that represents an open building opportunity rather than an already-solved problem.
insights.locator: Panellists said interoperability remained a major gap

insights.claim: Closing priorities from the panel included shared community/civic data-governance tooling, mesh networking, and consolidating around a smaller set of shared building blocks (identity, encryption) instead of every project rebuilding primitives from scratch, with Boris Mann specifically flagging JMAP as a standard worth watching for contacts, calendar, and email interoperability.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: A concrete, actionable watchlist item (JMAP) and a broader call for shared primitives that could reduce fragmentation and duplicated engineering effort across local-first projects.
insights.locator: Mann said the community should focus less on repeatedly rebuilding identity and encryption
